title: Strings are Arrays 🔤
description: Discover how C fundamentally handles text.

slides.title: The String Illusion 🪄
slides.content:
We've mentioned this before: **C does not have a native `String` type.**

When you see text like `"Hello"` in C, it's an illusion. It is actually just an **array of `char` variables** stored sequentially in memory: `['H', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o']`.

slides.title: Creating a String
slides.content:
Because strings are arrays, you create them exactly like arrays.

Notice the `%s` format specifier, which tells printf to "keep printing characters until the string ends."

slides.title: The Null Terminator (\0) 🛑
slides.content:
Did you see that weird `\0` in the first method? That is called the **Null Terminator**.

Because C strings are just bare arrays, the computer needs a way to know exactly where the word stops. `\0` is an invisible zero-byte marker placed at the very end of EVERY string.

It tells `printf`: *"Stop printing characters! The word is finished!"*

quizData.question: What happens if a string is missing its Null Terminator `\0`?

options.text: Functions like `printf` will just keep reading and printing random memory forever until they accidentally hit a 0 byte or crash.

quizData.explanation: Without the stop sign (`\0`), C has absolutely no idea the string has ended.

quizData.question: If you define `char name[] = "Bob";`, exactly how many bytes of memory does that array consume?
quizData.explanation: It takes 3 bytes for 'B', 'o', 'b' + 1 extra invisible byte at the end for the `\0` Null Terminator!
